name: Skinner Mine
short_description: Historic phosphate mining area near Jacksonville offering opportunities to collect quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens. Flat, accessible terrain makes this a good beginner location.
description:
Skinner Mine is a historic mining site in Duval County near Jacksonville, Florida. The location sits in the Atlantic Coastal Plain at very low elevation (59 ft), characterized by flat terrain typical of northern Florida's landscape. This area has a history connected to Florida's phosphate mining industry, which extracted minerals from Cenozoic-age deposits. Today, the site offers rockhounders access to quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens that can be found in the exposed earth and tailings areas.

The geology here reflects Florida's position on the trailing edge of the North American continent. During the Miocene and Pliocene epochs, marine and fluvial sediments deposited layers rich in phosphate and silicate minerals. Subsequent weathering and erosion have exposed these mineral-bearing strata, creating collecting opportunities for patient rockhounders. The flat topography means minimal elevation change and easy walking across the site.

Expect to find small quartz chips, feldspar fragments, and mica flakes scattered across disturbed ground. The material is typically worn and weathered, reflecting the site's geological age and exposure to Florida's humid subtropical climate. Success here requires careful scanning of the ground surface and small excavation with hand tools in permitted areas.

Because this is a historic mining area, always verify current land status and ownership before visiting. Some portions may be private property or restricted. Check with local authorities or the property owner for current access permissions and any collection limitations.
history: Skinner Mine is situated within Florida's historic phosphate mining belt. During the late 19th and 20th centuries, phosphate mining transformed northern Florida's landscape, extracting valuable deposits laid down during Miocene and Pliocene marine transgression events. The Hawthorn Group formations exposed at historic mine sites like Skinner contain phosphate nodules, quartz, feldspar, and other silicate minerals that accumulated in shallow marine and estuarine environments tens of millions of years ago.
tips: Search the ground surface and exposed banks carefully for quartz nodules and feldspar fragments - these often concentrate in areas of prior excavation. Use a small hand shovel or digging tool to gently work through loose, weathered material in permitted zones; avoid disturbing intact rock faces. Look for translucent or milky quartz pieces and colorless to pink feldspar crystals, typically finger-sized or smaller. Bring a classifier or mesh screen to separate finer mica flakes and smaller specimens from clay and sand.
terrain_notes: Elevation 59 feet with completely flat terrain. Ground surface is typically loose sand, clay, and weathered rock from historic mining disturbance. Vegetation sparse to moderate depending on reclamation. Walking distances from parking vary by site conditions; expect firm footing on disturbed ground but potentially soft or boggy areas in depressions. Humidity and heat can be significant during warmer months. No dramatic relief or obstacles.
best_season: October - April. Florida's subtropical climate means hot and humid conditions May through September with afternoon thunderstorms common. Winter months offer cooler temperatures, lower humidity, and lower mosquito pressure, making field work more comfortable. Late fall through early spring also has less chance of flash flooding in low-lying areas.
written_directions: From Jacksonville, take US 17 south toward the Duval County area near the GPS coordinates. Local roads in the vicinity include various county roads accessing former mining properties. Because Skinner Mine is a historic site with uncertain current access and ownership, contact the Duval County Property Appraiser's office or local land management to confirm access routes and parking locations before visiting. The site is approximately 10-15 miles south of downtown Jacksonville depending on exact access point.
road_conditions: Local roads are paved county roads. Access to the actual mine site may be via unpaved drive or footpath depending on current ownership and conditions.
parking_notes: Parking is uncertain due to private ownership status. If access is granted, parking may be available near site entrance or pulloff areas. Confirm with property owner before visiting.
collection_rules: This is a private site. Permission from the property owner is required to enter and collect. Personal collection only - no commercial use. Collect only small hand specimens. Do not disturb structural features, machinery, or intact mine infrastructure. Stay on designated pathways if provided. Respect any posted signs and access restrictions.
quantity_limits: Personal use only - collect reasonable hand specimen quantities.
rules:
Permission from property owner required
Personal collection only
No commercial use

Stay on designated paths
Do not disturb mine infrastructure
Respect all posted signs and restrictions
nearest_services: Jacksonville, Florida - approximately 10-15 miles north. Full services available including gas, food, water, and emergency services.
accessibility_notes: Flat terrain with no elevation change makes this physically accessible to most visitors. Ground surface is sandy and loose in places, suitable for standard footwear. No wheelchair accessibility due to private property status and unimproved ground. Minimal walking required from parking area. Heat and humidity can be challenging in warm months.

faq.question: What can I find rockhounding at Skinner Mine near Jacksonville?
faq.answer: Skinner Mine offers opportunities to collect quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens. The historic phosphate mining site has exposed Miocene and Pliocene-age strata containing these silicate minerals. Quartz pieces are typically milky or translucent, feldspar is colorless to pink, and mica appears as thin flakes scattered across the disturbed ground.

faq.question: Is there a fee to rockhound at Skinner Mine in Florida?
faq.answer: Skinner Mine is private property, so there is no public fee dig operation. Access requires permission from the property owner. Contact local land records or the Duval County Property Appraiser's office to identify the current owner and request permission to visit and collect specimens.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Skinner Mine?
faq.answer: Because Skinner Mine is private property, you must obtain permission from the owner before entering. This is technically a private permit requirement. No government permit is needed, but trespassing without owner permission is illegal. Always verify access and ownership status before planning a visit.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound near Jacksonville at Skinner Mine?
faq.answer: The best season is October through April when Florida's weather is cooler and more comfortable. Summer months (May - September) bring extreme heat, humidity, afternoon thunderstorms, and high mosquito activity that make field work unpleasant. Winter collecting offers ideal temperatures and lower flood risk.

faq.question: Is Skinner Mine suitable for beginner rockhounders and families?
faq.answer: Yes, if access is granted, Skinner Mine is beginner-friendly because of its flat, easy terrain and accessible ground-surface collecting. No special climbing or hiking skills are required. Children ages 6 and up can participate. However, always supervise children around historic mine sites due to potential hazards like loose rock and abandoned infrastructure.

faq.question: What tools do I need for rockhounding at Skinner Mine?
faq.answer: Bring a small hand shovel or digging tool for gentle excavation in permitted areas, a mesh screen or classifier to separate finer specimens, and a collection bag. A magnifying glass helps identify feldspar and mica details. Hand tools are sufficient - motorized equipment is not allowed. Wear gloves to protect from sharp quartz edges and wear sturdy footwear.

meta_description: Collect quartz, feldspar, and mica at Skinner Mine near Jacksonville. Historic phosphate mining site with flat terrain. Permission required - private property.
